key_points: The document is an agenda item (File #25-1313) to accept an annual report prepared under the California Mitigation Fees Act.
The text confirms the existence of the Affordable Housing Impact Fee and Commercial Linkage Fee but provides no specific fee rates or cost data.
There is no evidence in the provided text regarding changes to development costs attributable to these fees.
There is no evidence in the provided text regarding the likelihood of cost-of-living pass-through.
Secondary research is required to answer the specific questions about development costs and economic pass-through.

It does not contain data on fee amounts, the impact on development costs, or potential cost-of-living pass-through.

top_results.title: City of San José - File #: 25-1313 - Calendar

top_results.snippet: The San José City Council voted on September 1, 2020 to begin charging commercial developers fees to fund affordable housing. The Commercial Linkage Fee is a one-time impact fee that applies to new and existing Non-Residential Projects in San José that either add gross floor area or change the existing building use. The San José City Council voted on December 11, 2018 to pursue a nexus and a feasibility study on a potential commercial linkage fee in San José. | Industrial/Research and Development (≥100,000 sq.ft.) | $3.58 - When paid in full prior to the Building Permit issuance, a 20% reduction in the payment will apply. | Industrial/Research and Development(≥100,000 sq.ft.) | $3.58 - When paid in full prior to the Building Permit issuance, a 20% reduction in the payment will apply. | Industrial/Research and Development (≥100,000 sq.ft.) | $3.58 - When paid in full prior to the Building Permit issuance, a 20% reduction in the payment will apply.
top_results.title: Commercial Linkage Fee | City of San José

top_results.snippet: # After years of debate, San Jose OKs commercial development fees for affordable housing. After years of debate, San Jose OKs commercial development fees for affordable housing. After more than five years of stalled talks, San Jose will begin charging commercial developers fees to fund affordable housing — a move housing advocates said is long overdue. Supporters of the fees say that as commercial developments, such as the massive Google campus planned for downtown, bring more jobs to the city, the need for additional affordable housing will increase. Sandy Perry, executive director of the Affordable Housing Network of Santa Clara County, called the proposed fees a public relations stunt and said San Jose leaders would be at the behest of Google and other developers if they adopted such a low fee.
top_results.title: After years of debate, San Jose OKs commercial development fees for affordable housing - San José Spotlight
